name: Islamic central lands: Rashidun → Umayyad → Abbasid → Mamluk → Ottoman (632–1922)
description: Five-link trunk of political hegemony over the central Islamic lands (the Fertile Crescent, Egypt, and later Anatolia), from the Rashidun caliphate at Medina and Kufa (632–661) through the Umayyad dynasty at Damascus (661–750), the Abbasid caliphate at Baghdad (750–1258), the Mamluk Sultanate at Cairo (1250–1517, hosting a shadow Abbasid caliphate from 1261), and the Ottoman empire (1299–1922) which assumed the caliphal title formally in 1517 with Selim I's conquest of Egypt. The chain tracks effective Sunni political and caliphal authority in the central Islamic lands; it is explicitly NOT the full landscape of Islamic political history (which includes the parallel Fatimid Shia caliphate in Cairo 909–1171, the Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba 756/929–1031, the Buyid, Samanid, Seljuk, Ghaznavid, and Khwarazmian intermediate powers, the Mongol Ilkhanate, and the Safavid empire, all of which exist at the entity level in AtlasPI but are not on this trunk).
ethical_notes: Five points of care that the trunk format tends to elide. (a) Every transition in this chain is violent: the First Fitna that founded the Umayyad line involved the assassination of Ali ibn Abi Talib at Kufa in 661 and the forced abdication of al-Hasan; the Umayyad–Husayn conflict culminated in the killing of the Prophet's grandson Husayn at Karbala in 680 (the foundational trauma of Shia Islam, commemorated annually at Ashura); the Abbasid Revolution of 750 ended at the battle of the Zab and with the 'Banquet of Abu Futrus' (June 750) at which Abbasid forces executed ~80 Umayyad princes invited under the pretext of amnesty — only Abd al-Rahman I escaped, reaching al-Andalus and founding the Cordoban Umayyad line; the Mongol sack of Baghdad in 1258 killed al-Musta'sim and an estimated 200,000–800,000 residents, with the Tigris running black with library ink and red with blood (Ibn al-Athir, al-Tabari's later continuators); Selim I's Egyptian campaign (Marj Dabiq 1516, Ridaniya 1517) killed the Mamluk sultan Qansuh al-Ghawri, captured and hanged his successor Tumanbay II at Bab Zuwayla in Cairo, and deported the last shadow Abbasid caliph al-Mutawakkil III to Istanbul. (b) The chain represents a Sunni trunk — it does not include the Shia line that branches at Ali (the twelve Imams of Twelver Shiism, the Ismaili Fatimid caliphate, the Zaydi imamates of Yemen), nor the Ibadi Omani imamate. Users consulting this chain for 'Islamic succession' generally must understand that Sunni–Shia–Ibadi divergence is itself the content of the First Fitna, and the trunk-format representation risks normalising only one of three valid continuities. (c) The Umayyad Caliphate of Córdoba (independent emirate from 756 under Abd al-Rahman I, formal caliphate from 929 to 1031) is a parallel Sunni caliphate not on this trunk; the Fatimid caliphate (909–1171) in Ifriqiya and later Egypt is a parallel Shia caliphate also not on this trunk. Both are entity-level records in AtlasPI. (d) The 'Mamluk' link compresses two very different dynasties — the Bahri Mamluks (1250–1382, Turkic-Kipchak) founded by the assassination of the last Ayyubid sultan al-Muazzam Turanshah in 1250, and the Burji Mamluks (1382–1517, Circassian) — into a single entity; both periods involved regular succession-by-assassination (the Mamluk sultanate has one of the highest rates of violent succession in pre-modern history, with average sultanic reign length under 6 years). (e) The Ottoman caliphal claim was formalised in 1517 but the earlier caliphal succession — from the deported al-Mutawakkil III (who returned to Cairo and died 1543) to Selim and onward — is a contested legal question; formal Ottoman caliphal ideology crystallises only in the late 19th century under Abdulhamid II, who weaponised pan-Islamic caliphal claims against European colonial powers. Abolition of the caliphate by the Turkish Grand National Assembly on 3 March 1924 (separately from the 1922 abolition of the sultanate) ends 1,292 years of formal caliphal succession.

sources.citation: al-Tabari (d. 923). تاريخ الرسل والملوك (Tārīkh al-rusul wa-l-mulūk / History of the Prophets and Kings), 40 vols. Eng. tr. SUNY Press 1985–2007.
Ibn al-Athir (d. 1233). الكامل في التاريخ (al-Kāmil fī l-Tārīkh / The Complete History), esp. vols covering 1258 Baghdad sack.
al-Maqrizi (d. 1442). السلوك لمعرفة دول الملوك (Kitāb al-sulūk li-maʿrifat duwal al-mulūk) — primary for Mamluk-era Egypt.
Ibn Khaldun (d. 1406). العبر (Kitāb al-ʿIbar / The Muqaddimah and Universal History).
Kennedy, H. (2004). The Prophet and the Age of the Caliphates. 2nd ed. Pearson Longman.
Kennedy, H. (2007). The Great Arab Conquests. Da Capo.
Hoyland, R. (2015). In God's Path: The Arab Conquests and the Creation of an Islamic Empire. Oxford UP.
Hodgson, M. (1974). The Venture of Islam, 3 vols. Chicago UP — canonical periodisation.
Berkey, J. (2003). The Formation of Islam: Religion and Society in the Near East, 600–1800. Cambridge UP.
Crone, P. (1980). Slaves on Horses: The Evolution of the Islamic Polity. Cambridge UP.
Madelung, W. (1997). The Succession to Muḥammad: A Study of the Early Caliphate. Cambridge UP.
Morgan, D. (1986, 2nd ed. 2007). The Mongols. Blackwell — for 1258 Baghdad.
Irwin, R. (1986). The Middle East in the Middle Ages: The Early Mamluk Sultanate 1250–1382. Croom Helm.
Finkel, C. (2005). Osman's Dream: The History of the Ottoman Empire. Basic Books — for 1517 caliphal transfer.

links.description: The Rashidun ('rightly guided') caliphate — the four successors of Muhammad recognised by Sunni tradition: Abu Bakr (632–634), Umar ibn al-Khattab (634–644), Uthman ibn Affan (644–656), and Ali ibn Abi Talib (656–661). Capital at Medina; under Umar and Uthman the Arab conquests take Sasanian Iran (by 651) and Byzantine Syria, Egypt, and North Africa (by 660s). The period ends with the First Fitna (656–661), Islam's first civil war.
links.ethical_notes: The 'Rashidun' designation is itself a Sunni theological claim of exemplary legitimacy, not a neutral political description. Shia traditions hold that only Ali was legitimate (the first three being usurpers); Ibadi traditions diverge after Uthman. Three of the four Rashidun caliphs were assassinated: Umar (stabbed by the Persian slave Abu Lu'lu'a in Medina, 644), Uthman (besieged and killed in his house by Egyptian-Kufan rebels, 656), and Ali (struck with a poisoned sword by the Kharijite Ibn Muljam at Kufa mosque, 661). The 'Rightly Guided' label papers over a period of escalating civil violence culminating in the First Fitna and the founding schism of Islam.

links.description: The First Fitna (656–661) and Muawiyah ibn Abi Sufyan's seizure of the caliphate. After the Battle of Siffin (657) between Ali's forces and Muawiyah's Syrian army ends inconclusively, Ali's authority fractures (the Kharijite schism), Ali is assassinated at Kufa in January 661 by the Kharijite Ibn Muljam, al-Hasan ibn Ali abdicates to Muawiyah in exchange for a pension and peace, and Muawiyah proclaims himself caliph at Damascus — founding the Umayyad dynasty (661–750) and moving the caliphal capital from Medina/Kufa to Damascus. Under the Umayyads the conquests continue: Sind (711), al-Andalus (711–718), the Maghreb (to the Atlantic), Central Asia to the Syr Darya, and the height of territorial extent by ~730. The Umayyads institute Arab-tribal aristocratic rule over non-Arab mawali (clients), which will generate the social tensions that power the Abbasid Revolution.
links.ethical_notes: The Umayyad transition is violent at its origin (assassination of Ali; coerced abdication of al-Hasan, who is later poisoned in 670 — Shia tradition attributes the poisoning to Muawiyah's agents) and continues: the killing of Husayn ibn Ali at Karbala on 10 Muharram 61 AH / 10 October 680 — the Prophet's grandson, his family, and ~70 companions besieged and killed by Yazid I's army — is the foundational trauma of Shia Islam, commemorated annually at Ashura. The Umayyad caliphate was characterised by Arab-tribal exclusivism (the dhimmi jizya was heavy, mawali were structurally disadvantaged) which the Abbasid revolution would weaponise. The Karbala atrocity alone is sufficient reason not to treat the Rashidun→Umayyad transition as 'succession'.

links.description: The Abbasid Revolution (747–750): the Abbasid faction, descendants of Muhammad's uncle al-Abbas, organise a revolutionary movement in Khurasan around Abu Muslim, exploiting Arab-mawali discontent with Umayyad rule. Abbasid black banners (the eponymous al-musawwida) defeat the Umayyad caliph Marwan II at the Battle of the Zab (January 750); Marwan flees to Egypt and is killed; Abu'l-Abbas al-Saffah ('the Blood-shedder') is proclaimed caliph at Kufa on 28 November 749. The Abbasids move the caliphal capital from Damascus to Kufa, then to al-Hashimiyya, and finally to the purpose-built Baghdad (Madinat al-Salam, founded 762 under al-Mansur). The Abbasid caliphate (750–1258) becomes the canonical Sunni caliphate, presiding over the Islamic Golden Age (Bayt al-Hikma, translation movement, al-Khwarizmi, Razi, Avicenna) but losing effective political power progressively to Turkish military slaves (from the 860s, Samarra period) and to regional dynasties (Buyids from 945, Seljuks from 1055).
links.ethical_notes: The Abbasid seizure included systematic extermination of the Umayyad royal family: the infamous 'Banquet of Abu Futrus' in June 750, at which ~80 Umayyad princes were invited to a reconciliation banquet at the estate of Abu Futrus (near Yafo/Jaffa) and massacred. The only prince to escape, Abd al-Rahman ibn Mu'awiya (the future Abd al-Rahman I), fled to al-Andalus and founded the Cordoban Umayyad line. Abbasid ideology rhetorically centred piety and just rule but the dynasty developed mechanisms of violent succession (regular caliphal blindings, harem politics, Turkish military kingmakers from the 860s) that the Sunni legal tradition would retrospectively naturalise. The Islamic Golden Age (the standard ideological framing) coincided with the expansion of the Zanj slave trade from East Africa to Abbasid Mesopotamia — the Zanj Rebellion (869–883) was led by enslaved East Africans in southern Iraq and killed an estimated 500,000 before its suppression. The Golden Age narrative systematically underplays slavery and slave revolt.

links.description: The Mongol sack of Baghdad (29 January – 10 February 1258) under Hulagu Khan: after the caliph al-Musta'sim refuses to submit, Hulagu's army (including Georgian and Christian Armenian auxiliaries eager for revenge on Muslim powers) takes the city, kills al-Musta'sim (rolled in a carpet and trampled by horses, per Ibn al-Athir's continuators, so that no noble blood touched the ground per Mongol taboo), and conducts a week-long sack with estimated civilian casualties of 200,000–800,000 (Ibn al-Athir's figure is 800,000; modern estimates vary widely). The House of Wisdom is destroyed; the Tigris runs with ink from the thrown books. The Mamluk Sultanate at Cairo, led by Baybars, defeats the Mongols at Ain Jalut (3 September 1260) and halts the westward advance; in 1261 Baybars installs Ahmad ibn al-Zahir, a cousin of al-Musta'sim, as the first 'shadow' Abbasid caliph in Cairo, providing symbolic caliphal continuity under Mamluk military protection. This shadow caliphate persists until 1517. The Mamluk sultanate (1250 founding with the murder of the last Ayyubid Turanshah by Bahri Mamluks under Shajar al-Durr and Aybak; dynastic periods Bahri 1250–1382 and Burji 1382–1517) rules Egypt, Syria, and the Hejaz; it also halts the Mongol advance at Ain Jalut and Homs (1281) and expels the last Crusader states (Acre, 1291).
links.ethical_notes: The 1258 sack is recorded in Ibn al-Athir's continuator and in the Syriac Chronicle of Bar Hebraeus — the numbers are contested (modern scholarship, Morgan 1986, treats 800,000 as probably exaggerated but acknowledges massive real mortality) but the destruction of Baghdad's scholarly infrastructure is archaeologically attested. The Mamluk Sultanate itself was founded by regicide (murder of Turanshah 1250) and sustained through a kind of institutionalised violent succession — the average sultanic reign over 267 years was under six years, with ~40% of sultans dying by assassination or violent deposition. The 'shadow caliphate' in Cairo was a Mamluk legitimation instrument: the Cairo Abbasid caliphs held no political power, were appointed and deposed at the will of the Mamluk sultans, and their function was to issue ritual investiture documents legitimising Mamluk rule. Users should not read the 1261 shadow caliphate as a genuine continuation of Abbasid political authority.

links.description: Selim I's Egyptian campaign (1516–1517): after the Battle of Chaldiran (1514) crushes Safavid resistance in the east, Selim turns south against the Mamluk Sultanate, defeats and kills the elderly Mamluk sultan Qansuh al-Ghawri at the Battle of Marj Dabiq (24 August 1516, north of Aleppo — Mamluk cavalry overrun by Ottoman matchlock musketry and artillery), takes Aleppo and Damascus, then defeats the last Mamluk sultan Tumanbay II at the Battle of Ridaniya outside Cairo (22 January 1517). Tumanbay escapes briefly, is captured, and on Selim's order is hanged at Bab Zuwayla in Cairo on 13 April 1517. Selim deports the last shadow Abbasid caliph, al-Mutawakkil III, to Istanbul; Ottoman ideology later claims al-Mutawakkil formally transferred the caliphate to Selim (the 'ceremonial sword' narrative at the Eyüp mosque) — historical reality is that al-Mutawakkil returned to Cairo in 1520 and died there in 1543 as a pensioner. The formal Ottoman caliphate as a political-religious claim crystallises under Abdulhamid II (r. 1876–1909) in the late 19th century as a response to European colonial expansion over Muslim populations. The Ottoman caliphate is formally abolished by the Turkish Grand National Assembly on 3 March 1924 (Law no. 431), ending 1,292 years of formal Sunni caliphal succession.
links.ethical_notes: The Ottoman conquest of 1516–1517 killed an estimated 80,000 Mamluk soldiers and many thousands of Syrian and Egyptian civilians, though exact totals are poorly documented in the sources (Finkel 2005 notes the Ottoman chronicles emphasise Selim's clemency to Cairo's population while Mamluk-tradition sources emphasise plunder). The hanging of Tumanbay II at Bab Zuwayla was a deliberately public humiliation-execution of a legitimate Muslim sovereign. The claim of 'caliphal transfer' from al-Mutawakkil III to Selim is a late-Ottoman ideological construct; the 19th-century caliphal revival under Abdulhamid II was used to project pan-Islamic legitimacy against European colonialism (over British India, Dutch East Indies, Russian Central Asia) and had real geopolitical effects — including the 1914 Ottoman declaration of jihad against the Allied powers, which failed to mobilise Indian and Central Asian Muslims as the Young Turks had hoped. Abolition by Atatürk's Grand National Assembly was a rupture not just for Turkey but for the transnational Islamic political imaginary; it motivated subsequent attempts to revive the caliphal office (King Hussein of the Hejaz 1924, the Cairo Caliphal Conference 1926, and — in a vastly different and violent register — the Islamic State's 2014 proclamation of a 'caliphate'). The 1924 abolition is thus the endpoint of this chain and a foundational moment for contemporary Islamist politics.
